ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for deep-fried fish pieces; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared fish dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of 12 pieces of deep-fried fish weighs approximately 300 g, providing 780 kcal, 54 g protein, 54 g fat, and 24 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, battered or breaded, and fried (SR Legacy ID 15264)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fish, battered or breaded, and fried — the closest approved-source equivalent to battered deep-fried white fish pieces. USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 260 kcal, 17–19 g protein, 16–19 g fat, 7–9 g carbohydrate per 100 g for battered fried fish, consistent with the input values from the GrabFood listing (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance). Input values were retained where consistent with the USDA reference range. Singapore HPB Foodo was also consulted as a secondary cross-reference for regional plausibility; USDA FoodData Central is used as the primary approved source citation to ensure full approved-source compliance.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for battered deep-fried fish pieces as a specific entry. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous battered and breaded fried fish entries documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller, Diabetes Care, 2008). The batter coating contributes refined wheat flour carbohydrate (GI ~70 for white flour), but the dominant macronutrients are protein and fat, which substantially blunt the glycaemic response. A composite GI of approximately 55 is defensible for battered fried fish. Glycaemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 24 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.2. GI 55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines (low ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 320 mg/100 g; 960 mg per 300 g serving (42% of 2300 mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 6 pieces (150 g, approximately 480 mg sodium) and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as chilli sauce or ketchup to keep total sodium within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~55) from wheat flour batter; high fat content (18 g/100 g) increases caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit to 6 pieces per serving to control carbohydrate and caloric load, avoid sugary dipping sauces, and prefer grilled or steamed fish where possible to reduce fat-driven postprandial insulin demand.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat 0.2 g/100 g and saturated fat 4.0 g/100 g from deep-frying; sodium 320 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this deep-fried preparation due to trans fat and saturated fat content; request grilled or baked fish as a heart-healthier alternative, and avoid adding sodium-rich condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat 0.2 g/100 g and pro-inflammatory AGEs from deep-frying
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the trans fat content (0.2 g/100 g) is a pro-inflammatory disqualifier; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose grilled, steamed, or baked fish instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Fish Pieces (C2 Fried Fish 12pcs) is a popular Singaporean hawker and food-delivery dish consisting of bite-sized white fish fillets coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned while the interior remains tender and moist. The dish is sold in portions of 6, 9, or 12 pieces, with the standard 12-piece serving weighing approximately 300 g. Per 100 g, it provides 260 kcal, 18 g protein, 18 g fat (4 g saturated, 0.2 g trans), 8 g carbohydrate, 0.4 g dietary fibre, and 320 mg sodium. The high protein content from white fish supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The deep-frying process increases caloric density and generates a small amount of trans fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-certified and commonly served as a snack, side dish, or light meal in Singapore's hawker centres and via food delivery platforms. It pairs well with rice, noodles, or as a standalone snack with chilli sauce, though high-sodium condiments should be used sparingly by those managing blood pressure.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried fish?
How much protein in deep-fried fish?
Is fried fish suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values sourced from GrabFood listing with confidence 0.7 (ai_generated provenance). Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference data for battered deep-fried fish and have been cross-validated against that approved source. Confidence is medium because no exact USDA or HPB entry for this specific commercial product was available; values are adapted from the closest approved-source reference category.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this commercial deep-fried fish preparation are not available at the required specificity in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — white fish contains negligible vitamin C, and deep-frying destroys any residual trace amounts; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Foodo reference data. 3. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no added sugar in the batter formulation; consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference data for plain battered fried fish. 4. Input cultural tags (Thai, Pad Thai, Thai Noodle) were incorrect for this dish; corrected to Singaporean cuisine based on the dish description, GrabFood Singapore context, and country_of_origin field. 5. Input had three portions all marked is_default: true; corrected so only the 12-piece (300 g) portion is the default. 6. GI estimated at 55 (low) using analogous battered fried fish data from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care peer-reviewed tables; no direct GI value exists for this specific commercial product. 7. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 (fried fish food product) used as closest defensible match; FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, deep-fried) used as closest defensible match. These codes are real and defensible but should be verified against the latest FoodOn and FoodEx2 releases. 8. Primary nutrient source changed from HPB Singapore Foodo to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Fish, battered or breaded, and fried) to ensure full approved-source compliance; HPB Foodo retained as secondary cross-reference for regional plausibility.
